source_intake_record.raw_question_text: As shown in the figure, $AD$ is the perpendicular bisector of $BC$ with the foot of the perpendicular as $D$, $\angle BAC=45^\circ$, $CE\perp AB$ at $E$, intersecting $AD$ at $F$, and $BD=2$. What is the length of $AF$?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text:
\textbf{Solution:} Since $AD$ is the perpendicular bisector of $BC$, and $BD=2$, \\
thus, $BC=2BD=4$, \\
Since $CE\perp AB$, \\
thus, $\angle AEC=90^\circ$, \\
Since $\angle BAC=45^\circ$, \\
thus, $\angle ACE=90^\circ-45^\circ=45^\circ$, \\
thus, $\angle EAC=\angle ACE$, \\
thus, $AE=CE$, \\
Since $AB=AC$, and point $D$ is the midpoint of $BC$, \\
thus, $AD\perp BC$, \\
thus, $\angle ADB=90^\circ$, \\
thus, $\angle B+\angle BAD=90^\circ$, \\
Since $\angle B+\angle BCE=90^\circ$, \\
thus, $\angle BAD=\angle BCE$, \\
In $\triangle AEF$ and $\triangle CEB$, \\
$\left\{\begin{array}{l} \angle AEF=\angle CEB \\ AE=CE \\ \angle EAF=\angle BCE \end{array}\right.$, \\
thus, $\triangle AEF\cong \triangle CEB$ (ASA), \\
thus, $AF=BC=\boxed{4}$,

decision_reasons: question, image, and answer are all present, so the core semantic assets are complete
asset registry passed with no reported issues
no risk flags are present in the initial scoring record
image dependency is high, and the required image asset exists
verifiability is reasonably strong because an answer is available

normalization_record.normalized_question_text: As shown in the figure, $AD$ is the perpendicular bisector of $BC$ with foot $D$, $\angle BAC=45^\circ$, $CE\perp AB$ at $E$, and $CE$ intersects $AD$ at $F$. If $BD=2$, what is the length of $AF$?
normalization_record.normalized_answer_text:
Since $AD$ is the perpendicular bisector of $BC$ and $BD=2$, thus $BC=2BD=4$.
Since $CE\perp AB$, thus $\angle AEC=90^\circ$.
Since $\angle BAC=45^\circ$, thus $\angle ACE=90^\circ-45^\circ=45^\circ$, so $\angle EAC=\angle ACE$, and hence $AE=CE$.
Since $AB=AC$, and point $D$ is the midpoint of $BC$, thus $AD\perp BC$, so $\angle ADB=90^\circ$, and $\angle B+\angle BAD=90^\circ$.
Since $\angle B+\angle BCE=90^\circ$, thus $\angle BAD=\angle BCE$.
In $\triangle AEF$ and $\triangle CEB$,
$\left\{\begin{array}{l} \angle AEF=\angle CEB \\ AE=CE \\ \angle EAF=\angle BCE \end{array}\right.$,
thus, $\triangle AEF\cong \triangle CEB$ (ASA), and therefore $AF=BC=\boxed{4}$.

lightly cleaned wording and punctuation in question and answer without changing semantics

open_ended_problem_variants.rewritten_question_text: As shown in the figure, $AD$ is the perpendicular bisector of $BC$ with foot $D$, $\angle BAC=45^\circ$, $CE\perp AB$ at $E$, and $CE$ intersects $AD$ at $F$. If $BD=2$, what is the length of $AF$?
open_ended_problem_variants.expected_answer:
Since $AD$ is the perpendicular bisector of $BC$ and $BD=2$, thus $BC=2BD=4$.
Since $CE\perp AB$, thus $\angle AEC=90^\circ$.
Since $\angle BAC=45^\circ$, thus $\angle ACE=90^\circ-45^\circ=45^\circ$, so $\angle EAC=\angle ACE$, and hence $AE=CE$.
Since $AB=AC$, and point $D$ is the midpoint of $BC$, thus $AD\perp BC$, so $\angle ADB=90^\circ$, and $\angle B+\angle BAD=90^\circ$.
Since $\angle B+\angle BCE=90^\circ$, thus $\angle BAD=\angle BCE$.
In $\triangle AEF$ and $\triangle CEB$,
$\left\{\begin{array}{l} \angle AEF=\angle CEB \\ AE=CE \\ \angle EAF=\angle BCE \end{array}\right.$,
thus, $\triangle AEF\cong \triangle CEB$ (ASA), and therefore $AF=BC=\boxed{4}$.
